Context: Define domain-specific error types with automatic Error trait implementation
Do: Use #[derive(Error)] with #[error()] format strings and #[from] for automatic conversion
Dont: Manually implement Display and Error for every error type

Context: Add context to errors as they propagate up the call stack
Do: Use .context() or .with_context(|| ...) to add human-readable context to any error
Dont: Return bare errors with no context making debugging difficult

Context: Propagate errors concisely using the ? operator
Do: Use ? to propagate errors; implement From conversions so ? works across error types
Dont: Use verbose match blocks or .unwrap() to handle every error manually

Context: Create a custom error struct with source chaining for rich context
Do: Implement Error with source() method to enable error chain traversal; use #[source] with thiserror
Dont: Store inner errors as Strings losing the ability to inspect the error chain

Context: Check if an error in the chain is a specific type
Do: Use .downcast_ref::<T>() to check for specific error types in the chain; walk source() for nested errors
Dont: Use string matching on error messages for control flow

Context: Chain Result methods to transform values and errors without nested match blocks
Do: Use .map(), .map_err(), .and_then(), .unwrap_or_else() for clean error transformation pipelines
Dont: Use deeply nested match blocks for every Result transformation

Context: Distinguish between recoverable errors (Result) and unrecoverable bugs (panic)
Do: Use Result for any error a caller can handle; only panic for programmer bugs and invariant violations
Dont: Use .unwrap() on Results from user input or I/O that can legitimately fail

Keywords: Box, dyn Error, trait object, erased
Context: Use Box<dyn Error> as a quick error type for prototyping or when you need type-erased errors
Do: Use Box<dyn std::error::Error + Send + Sync> for quick prototypes; migrate to thiserror/anyhow for production code
Dont: Use Box<dyn Error> as the long-term error type in library APIs where callers need to match on variants

Context: Implement From to enable automatic error conversion with the ? operator
Do: Implement From<SourceError> for TargetError or use #[from] with thiserror
Dont: Chain .map_err() everywhere when From conversion would be cleaner

Context: Collect and return multiple validation errors at once
Do: Accumulate all field errors into a Vec and return them together
Dont: Return on the first validation error forcing users to fix one field at a time

Context: Return structured JSON error responses from HTTP handlers
Do: Define an error type that implements IntoResponse with proper status codes and JSON body
Dont: Return plain text errors or inconsistent error shapes

Context: Log errors once at the boundary and propagate them everywhere else
Do: Return errors up the stack and log them in one place such as middleware or main
Dont: Log the same error at every layer creating duplicate noisy log entries

Context: Provide meaningful panic messages with .expect() when unwrap is justified
Do: Use .expect("descriptive message") that explains why the value should be present
Dont: Use bare .unwrap() with no explanation of the invariant
Code Bad: let config_dir = dirs::config_dir().unwrap();\n// thread 'main' panicked at 'called `Option::unwrap()` on a `None` value'\n// no clue what went wrong or why it was expected to succeed

Context: Use TryFrom for fallible type conversions that may fail with a well-typed error
Do: Implement TryFrom<Source> for Target when conversion can fail; the TryInto impl is provided automatically
Dont: Return generic errors from conversion or use as for lossy numeric casts
Code Bad: let p = value as u8; // silent truncation for values > 255\n// no validation, silently wraps\nstruct Percentage(u8); // can hold invalid values like 200

Context: Use the never type (!) or Infallible to indicate operations that cannot fail
Do: Use Infallible as the Error type in TryFrom when conversion is infallible; use ! or unreachable!() for exhaustive matches
Dont: Leave an empty error type or use () which provides no semantic meaning

Context: Catch panics at thread or FFI boundaries to prevent undefined behavior and convert them to Results
Do: Use std::panic::catch_unwind at FFI boundaries and thread pool workers; convert panics to errors
Dont: Let panics propagate across FFI boundaries which is undefined behavior

Context: Use eyre/color-eyre for rich error reports with backtraces and span traces in applications
Do: Use color-eyre for CLI tools and applications where human-readable error reports matter
Dont: Use eyre in library code where callers need typed errors

Context: Handle errors correctly in async contexts where errors must be Send + Sync
Do: Ensure error types are Send + Sync + 'static for use across await points; use anyhow or thiserror
Dont: Use Rc<dyn Error> or non-Send errors in async code which prevents spawning tasks

Context: Define well-known error values that callers can match against
Do: Use specific error enum variants or constants that callers can pattern match on
Dont: Use generic string errors that callers must match with string comparison

Context: Provide fallback values for errors that have sensible defaults
Do: Use .unwrap_or(), .unwrap_or_else(), or .unwrap_or_default() for errors with known fallbacks
Dont: Use .unwrap() and rely on panic for values that have sensible defaults
Code Bad: let port: u16 = std::env::var("PORT").unwrap().parse().unwrap();\n// panics if PORT is unset or not a number\n// should use a default value
